_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Harnett County, NC. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses for a county formed in 1855 from Cumberland.
display_name: Harnett County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.fate: County seat moved to Lillington in 1882. The Toomer courthouse was a small frame structure. Records were transferred to Lillington.
Replaced by the current 1939 WPA-era building.

records_destroyed: Some loose court papers potentially disrupted during passage of troops
record_losses.description: Sherman's army crossed the Cape Fear near Lillington in March 1865, but the courthouse — then at Toomer — was not targeted. Harnett County was only ten years old and had limited infrastructure. No significant record losses are documented from the Civil War period.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Harnett County's records from its 1855 formation survive in good condition. If {ancestor_name}'s records are missing, the county's young age at the time of the Civil War means limited pre-war documentation, not destruction.
record_losses.source: Barrett, John G. Sherman's March Through the Carolinas. UNC Press, 1956.

deeds.completeness: Good from formation. Pre-1855 records for this territory are in Cumberland County.
deeds.repository: Harnett County Register of Deeds, Lillington; NC State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com NC land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good from formation. Pre-1855 estates are in Cumberland County.
wills_estates.repository: Harnett County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives

marriages.gaps: Pre-1855 marriages are in Cumberland County records.
marriages.repository: Harnett County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.gaps: None known. Early volumes are thin, reflecting small population.
court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; Harnett County Clerk of Court

vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives

substitute_sources.description: Harnett County was carved from Cumberland in 1855. All records for this territory before that date are in the Cumberland County courthouse in Fayetteville.
substitute_sources.repository: Cumberland County Register of Deeds, Fayetteville; NC State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for any Harnett County family with roots before 1855. Cumberland's records survive from 1754, so deep colonial-era documentation is available.
substitute_sources.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records.

substitute_sources.description: The Erwin Cotton Mills (Duke family) maintained employment records, payroll ledgers, and company housing records. Some survive in scattered archives.
substitute_sources.repository: Duke University Rubenstein Library (Duke family papers); NC State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Mill workers who appear in no other county records may be documented in company employment records. Housing assignments can confirm residence.
substitute_sources.source: Duke University finding aids, Rubenstein Library.

narrative_hooks: Harnett County was carved from Cumberland in 1855 — if {ancestor_name}'s family was here before that date, the records are in Fayetteville, in Cumberland County's courthouse, not in Lillington.

The Erwin Cotton Mills kept their own records of the workers in their company houses — if {ancestor_name} worked at the mill after 1892, {pronoun_possessive} name may survive in Duke family business papers as well as in the county records.
Harnett County's records survived the Civil War intact — the county was barely ten years old when Sherman passed through, and there was nothing strategic enough to burn.

Before 1913, no birth or death certificates exist for Harnett County — if {ancestor_name} was born or died before that date, church records, cemetery transcriptions, and Cumberland County's pre-1855 records are the alternatives.
source_references: Barrett, John G. Sherman's March Through the Carolinas. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1956.
NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
Leary, Helen F. M., ed. North Carolina Research: Genealogy and Local History. 2nd ed. Raleigh: North Carolina Genealogical Society, 1996.
